questions.prompt: What is the core decision ThreatLocker's Application Control makes when a user attempts to run a binary?
choices: Does this binary's signature match a known-bad sample?
Has this binary been observed exhibiting malicious behaviour?
Is this binary on the customer's allowlist?
Is this binary signed by a Microsoft certificate?
questions.explanation: ThreatLocker is a default-deny allowlisting product. The decision is whether the binary is explicitly permitted, not whether it's recognised as malicious. That's how it stops never-before-seen ransomware.

questions.prompt: ThreatLocker is an EDR product and replaces the customer's existing EDR or antivirus.
questions.explanation: ThreatLocker is a policy engine that prevents execution of unapproved binaries and constrains what approved apps can do. It runs alongside EDR / AV; the two pull different levers and are complementary, not substitutes.

questions.prompt: Word is on a customer's allowlist. The user opens a malicious .docx that tries to spawn PowerShell. What stops the attack?
choices: Application Control denies Word
A Ringfence on Word denies the spawn of PowerShell
ThreatLocker Detect raises an alert and remediates
The DNS resolver refuses to resolve the C2 domain
questions.explanation: Application Control permitted Word; the policy's Ringfence is what blocks Word from spawning PowerShell. Allowlisting decides what runs; ringfencing decides what running apps can do.

questions.prompt: Which portal area is where pending end-user approval requests live?
choices: Health Center
Unified Audit
Response Center
Modules
questions.explanation: The Response Center is the queue of pending approval requests. The Unified Audit logs what already happened; the Health Center shows agent and endpoint status.

questions.prompt: MSP staff should confirm the organisation in the portal header before approving any request for a customer.
questions.explanation: Approving in the wrong tenant is the costliest mistake at the ThreatLocker helpdesk. The portal header is the canonical check, and tab-switching can land you in a different org than you expect.

questions.prompt: If a request matches a ThreatLocker built-in application, you can safely approve it without further checks.
questions.explanation: A common DLL or system file may match into multiple unrelated built-ins. Approving a built-in via a request effectively permits the entire built-in. Confirm the requested file is genuinely part of that application before approving.

questions.prompt: When you approve a request, what's the default principle for choosing the scope of the resulting policy?
choices: Use 'Entire organisation' so the customer doesn't see this request again
Use the narrowest scope that solves the user's actual problem
Always use 'Computer group'
Match the scope to the requestor's access level
questions.explanation: Narrow first. Wider scopes mean the permit lives on more endpoints, which means a future compromise on any of them inherits the permit. Widen only when the customer explicitly needs it fleet-wide.

questions.prompt: Which of these signals on a request should slow you down before approving? (Select all that apply.)
choices: File path is c:\users\*\downloads\setup.exe with no certificate
File matches a built-in for a vendor the customer uses
Originating process is chrome.exe
Hash already on the customer's deny list
Requestor email doesn't match a real user account
questions.explanation: Unsigned downloads, hashes on the deny list, and bogus requestor emails are all yellow-or-red flags. A signed match for a vendor the customer uses, with chrome.exe as the originating process for a fresh download, is consistent with normal activity.

questions.prompt: Which of these are valid Action Type filters in the Unified Audit? (Select all that apply.)
choices: execute
network
elevate
scream
registry
questions.explanation: Documented Action Types include execute, install, network, registry, read, write, move, delete, baseline, powershell, elevate, configuration, dns. 'scream' isn't a thing.

questions.prompt: The Remove White Noise filter strips well-known white-noise denies from the Unified Audit so the signal is easier to find.
questions.explanation: The noise filter exists because the kernel pokes at common DLLs constantly. Without it, real denies are hidden in a wash of expected noise.

questions.prompt: A customer has clean role-based computer groups (Finance, Sales, Engineering) and people in each group share most software. Which Learning Mode flavour is most appropriate?
choices: Automatic Computer
Automatic Group
Automatic System
Tamper Protection Disabled
questions.explanation: Automatic Group creates new policies at the computer-group level, which is the right granularity when group members run the same software. Automatic Computer makes per-machine policies; Automatic System is drivers and Windows files only.

questions.prompt: Which of these are signs that a customer is NOT yet ready to flip from Learning Mode to Secured Mode? (Select all that apply.)
choices: New applications are still appearing in Learning every day
Power users have been through their normal weekly routine
The Unified Audit shows many green Simulated denies on Office and browsers
A subset of seasonal staff hasn't logged in during the Learning window
The application list looks complete to the customer's office manager
questions.explanation: Steady creation rate, lots of would-be-blocked Simulated denies, and dark-spot users mean the allowlist isn't representative yet. Completed routines and a customer-confirmed application list are 'ready' signals.

questions.prompt: A customer has been in Learning Mode for two weeks and the application list is still empty. What's the first thing to check?
choices: Whether the agent is actually checking in (Health Center)
Whether the customer's Default Deny policy is named in the format the automatic-creation logic looks for
Whether Learning Mode was scheduled with the correct end date
Whether the customer's plan SKU includes Application Control
questions.explanation: Vendor docs flag the Default Deny naming requirement as the most common Learning-Mode failure: if the catch-all Default policy has been renamed and no longer matches the expected pattern, automatic creation produces nothing even when everything else is configured. Check the policy name first.

questions.prompt: Why does the Simulated-deny count in the Unified Audit matter when judging cutover readiness?
choices: Each Simulated deny is a billing event the customer is charged for
It tells you how loud the Response Center will be on day one if you flip Secured now
The agent stops Learning automatically once the count crosses a threshold
Simulated denies indicate a configuration error that should block any cutover
questions.explanation: Simulated denies are the green-coloured rows where Secured Mode would have blocked the action. The count in the Learning window is the future post-cutover noise: a flood of Simulated denies on Office or browsers means the ringfences need tuning before you cut over, or the Response Center will be unmanageable on day one.

questions.prompt: During the pre-cutover review you find an old remote-access utility in the learned application list. Nobody at the customer remembers installing it or has a business reason to keep it. What's the right action?
choices: Leave the permit in place; uninstalling and re-permitting is double work
Remove the permit so the next launch attempt default-denies
Uninstall the software, then remove the permit so the next launch attempt default-denies
Move the permit to a Computer-Group level so it only affects one machine
questions.explanation: The discipline is uninstall-then-remove. Uninstalling without removing the permit leaves a stale allowlist entry that an attacker could later reuse. Removing the permit without uninstalling means the user gets denied next time and you get a ticket. Do both, in order, so the app is gone and the permit is gone.
